Executive Summary

Executive Summary

This is a high-pressure / VMC day for the KSQL–KBDN run — the Pacific storm track is well north, and the continental interior is dominated by a strong ridge by March 30. Primary Risk: forecast maturity is limited at 6 days out, and a new Pacific trough approaching the Northwest by Day 5–7 introduces uncertainty about exact timing of any wind or cloud increase near Bend. Provisional MARGINAL GO — the synoptic picture is favorable but not locked in at this range; the single most important variable to watch is whether that incoming Pacific system accelerates and clips central Oregon before your arrival window.

Operational Briefing

The Day Before — What to Watch

The evening of Sunday, March 29, pull the updated prog charts, any available TAFs for KSQL/KBDN, and the Pacific satellite loop. Specifically:

  • Check where the Pacific trough is. Day 5–6 extended progs (valid Mar 29–30) show a Low and associated front pushing into the Pacific Northwest. If that system is tracking faster than modeled, you'll see tightening isobars over Oregon and possible cloud/wind increases at Bend earlier than expected.
  • Check KBDN surface wind trend. Bend sits in a high desert bowl east of the Cascades. Prefrontal pressure gradients can drive strong westerly/southwesterly downslope flow off the Cascades with little warning. If winds at Redmond/Bend are already 20+ kt and gusty the evening before, expect worse by your arrival.
  • No-go trigger for that evening check: A TAF for KBDN showing BKN/OVC below 3,000 ft AGL or winds gusting above 25 kt should prompt a delay or replanning.

Leaving KSQL — Departure at 2026-03-30 14:00Z

TAFs for KSQL are not yet valid at this planning range — no specific ceiling or wind data available. That said, the synoptic picture for the Bay Area on March 30 looks like a ridge of high pressure in control, with the Pacific trough still well to the north (Day 6 prog). Departure from San Carlos should be VMC or very close to it.

Practically: Expect a standard coastal morning — possible marine layer at or near KSQL around 14:00Z (6 AM local), which is early enough that stratus could still be present near the Bay. This is terrain-local and not route-wide. If marine stratus is sitting at 1,500–2,500 ft over the Peninsula, it's a brief climb-through, not a sustained IMC problem — you'll break out well before reaching the Central Valley or Sierra foothills. LOCALIZED, CONDITIONAL.

No frontal boundary near the Bay Area at departure. Surface winds at KSQL are likely light and variable under high pressure, but confirm with the morning observation.

The Ride at 12,000 ft

Icing

No icing charts are available at this planning range (6+ days out). Based on the synoptic picture — ridge dominating the interior West, no significant QPF along the KSQL–KBDN corridor — widespread icing is not indicated. However:

  • At 12,000 ft MSL in late March over the Sierra/Oregon Cascades foothills, temperatures will likely be in the 0°C to -10°C range. That's the prime structural icing temperature band.
  • With no significant moisture forecast along the route (QPF charts show the interior West dry), icing risk is LOW CONFIDENCE / CONDITIONAL — it requires both cold temps AND moisture, and moisture isn't showing up for this corridor at this time.
  • Localized terrain clouds over the Cascades are always possible. If you're in visible moisture at 12,000 ft over terrain, treat any icing encounter as a divert trigger — descend toward the valley floor or climb above the layer if MEAs permit.
  • No SLD signal present. SLD cannot be assessed at this range without icing products, but no supporting evidence exists from QPF or moisture fields.
AltitudeIcing ProbabilityNotes
Below 8,000 ftLowBelow freezing level most of route; drier air
8,000–12,000 ftLow–ConditionalRight temps, but no significant moisture forecast
Above 12,000 ftLowLikely above significant cloud tops under ridge

Turbulence and Ride Quality

Winds aloft data is not yet available at this range. Based on the synoptic setup:

  • The Day 6 prog shows a high-pressure ridge over the interior West with relatively slack isobars over California and Oregon. En route winds aloft are likely moderate — probably 15–30 kt from the southwest at 12,000 ft, but this cannot be confirmed without winds aloft data.
  • Cascade crossing is the terrain hazard. Even under high pressure, westerly flow crossing the Cascades at 12,000 ft can produce light to moderate turbulence and mountain wave effects. This is LOCALIZED to the Cascade Range crossing, not route-wide. Operationally: expect a bumpy 20–30 minute stretch crossing the terrain, with possible brief altitude deviations.
  • If the Pacific trough has accelerated by departure day, winds aloft could be stronger (35–50 kt+), producing more significant wave/rotor. This is the primary uncertainty.
  • Timing note: 14:00Z departure is pre-noon local time — before significant daytime heating develops. This is actually good for minimizing convective and thermal turbulence over the valley. Not a significant factor on this route.

Altitude Strategy

  • Most favorable altitude band: 10,000–12,000 ft — above most terrain-level clouds, high enough to clear Cascade ridgelines, within range of comfortable oxygen territory for most pilots.
  • Least favorable: 8,000–10,000 ft over the Cascades — higher chance of being in cloud/terrain-level moisture and in the rotor zone if wave develops.
  • Escape option: If you pick up ice or encounter significant wave turbulence over the Cascades, descent toward the Deschutes Valley floor (Bend area, ~3,500 ft MSL) is your best option. Climbing above wave is difficult without oxygen and a capable aircraft.

Other Hazards

HazardRisk LevelLegWhat to Expect
Marine stratusLOCALIZED / LOWDeparture (KSQL climbout)Possible low ceiling near coast; brief climb-through
Mountain wave / turbulenceCONDITIONAL / MEDIUMCascade crossingLight-moderate chop; worse if Pacific trough accelerates

Getting Into KBDN

TAF for KBDN is not yet valid at this planning range. Estimated block time is approximately 2.5 hours; ETA ~16:30Z (8:30 AM local).

Under the current Day 6 synoptic picture, arrival at Bend should be VMC — the ridge is still in control, no precipitation forecast for central Oregon. Bend Muni sits at ~3,460 ft MSL in the high desert east of the Cascades. With a westerly/southwesterly wind regime, expect:

  • Estimated runway winds: Likely out of the west or southwest, potentially 10–20 kt with gusts possible — highly uncertain at this range. Runway 16/34 orientation at KBDN means a westerly wind could be a significant crosswind. Do not assume a direct headwind or tailwind without current data.
  • Terrain wind distortion: KBDN is downwind of the Cascades. Surface winds at the airport can differ substantially from winds aloft due to terrain channeling. Treat any runway wind estimate as approximate until you have actual TAF/METAR data.
  • If the Pacific system is closer: Ceilings could be dropping over the Cascades and potentially spreading east into the Bend area. This would tighten your margin quickly. Redmond (KRDM, ~16 nm north) is your best alternate — similar environment, ILS approach.

Best alternates: KRDM (Redmond, OR) is the primary. KLMT (Klamath Falls) is an option to the south if Bend goes below minimums.

Fly or No

MARGINAL GO — provisional, pending forecast updates within 24–48 hours of departure.

The synoptic picture at 6 days out is favorable — ridge over the interior, no significant moisture on the route, VMC departure and arrival expected. However, forecast maturity is limited at this range. The incoming Pacific trough visible in Day 5–7 progs introduces real uncertainty about conditions over the Oregon Cascades near your arrival time. This is not a hard no-go — it's a "watch and confirm" situation. File IFR for terrain structure and safety margin, not because IMC is expected.

What changes this call

  • KBDN TAF (once available) showing BKN/OVC below 3,000 ft AGL or winds gusting above 25 kt
  • Updated prog charts showing the Pacific trough over Oregon by March 30 (vs. still offshore) — escalates to NO-GO
  • Observed or forecast moderate-to-severe mountain wave SIGMETs for the Cascade Range at your altitude/time window
  • Icing PIREPs or FIP charts showing widespread icing above 10,000 ft over Oregon once those products are available (~48 hours out)

If You Go — Do This

  1. Night before (March 29): Pull updated prog charts, check Day 7 extended prog for Pacific trough position, get any available KBDN/KRDM TAFs, review winds aloft forecast for KSQL–KBDN at 12,000 ft. If trough has accelerated, reconsider.
  2. Morning of (March 30, 1–2 hours before departure): Check current METARs at KSQL, KSFO, KRDM, KBDN. Pull latest G-AIRMETs for IFR, turbulence, and icing. Check FIP icing charts for the 10,000–14,000 ft band over Oregon. Look for any SIGMETs over the Cascades.
  3. File IFR. Not because you're expecting IMC — but because the terrain, altitude, and potential for mountain wave make the IFR structure worth having. MEAs through the Cascades will be your friend if wave gets rough.
  4. Fuel: Top off at KSQL. Bend is not a fuel desert, but if you divert to Klamath Falls you want margin.
  5. Alternates filed: KRDM primary, KLMT secondary.
  6. Departure timing: 14:00Z (6 AM local) is solid — ahead of heating, ahead of any afternoon wind increase at Bend. Don't push departure later unless conditions clearly improve by doing so.
  7. In flight: Get a PIREP from anyone crossing the Cascades ahead of you. Check ATIS at KRDM before committing to KBDN if any doubt about arrival conditions.

Confidence

Confidence: LOW-MEDIUM

This is a 6-day forecast. The broad synoptic pattern — ridge over the interior West — is consistent across multiple prog runs, which is encouraging. But the specific timing and positioning of the incoming Pacific trough (Day 5–7 progs) carries significant uncertainty at this range. That trough's track determines whether you're flying under a clean ridge or into a deteriorating prefrontal environment over the Cascades. No TAFs, no winds aloft data, and no icing products are available yet to pin down the details.

The go/no-go picture will sharpen dramatically within 48 hours of departure. What you see now is a favorable but not confirmed scenario. Treat this as a provisional plan, not a committed go.
